Autonomous GPS Precision Delivery Drone

A fully autonomous quadcopter — designed, 3D printed, wired, and programmed from scratch — capable of navigating to any GPS coordinate and releasing a 4lb payload within half a meter of target. Built over 5 months for under $200.

BudgetUnder $200
Timeline5 Months
Drop Accuracy±0.5 m
Max Payload4 lbs
Flight Time~45 min
FrameFull PETG Print

01 — Overview

One drone.
One coordinate.
Half a meter.

The goal was deceptively simple: build a drone that flies itself to a GPS coordinate and drops a payload on target — for under $200. No shortcuts, no kit. Every structural component 3D printed in PETG. Every wire soldered by hand. Every parameter tuned from scratch.

Over 5 months, the project evolved through two full hardware iterations, each one exposing a new layer of complexity — flight dynamics, ArduPilot configuration, power systems, and the mechanical challenge of a precision release mechanism. The final result hit ±0.5 meter drop accuracy across 10 consecutive trials while carrying a 4lb payload and flying for 45 minutes.

02 — Version 1

V1: Learning to fly before learning to build

V1 used an off-the-shelf frame so the focus could go entirely on the electronics and software — the parts I knew the least about. This meant learning to solder for the first time: wiring ESCs, soldering XT60 connectors to a power distribution board, crimping signal wires to a flight controller, and understanding what each connection actually does before making it.

The flight controller is an STM32-based board running ArduPilot — an open-source autopilot stack used in real UAV applications. Getting ArduPilot configured from scratch meant learning how the software models aircraft dynamics: PID loops for attitude control, GPS fusion with the accelerometer and barometer, failsafe logic, and waypoint mission planning. None of it worked first try. Tuning the PIDs alone took weeks of test flights, crashing, adjusting, and flying again.

By the end of V1, the drone flew autonomously to waypoints reliably. But the frame was heavy, the legs snapped on hard landings, and there was no clean way to integrate a drop mechanism. Everything structural had to be redesigned.

03 — Version 2

V2: Print the whole thing

V2 scrapped the off-the-shelf frame entirely. The entire airframe — arms, center body, motor mounts, landing legs — was designed in SolidWorks and 3D printed in PETG. PETG was chosen for its balance of rigidity, layer adhesion, and impact resistance: better than PLA for a vehicle that lands hard and vibrates constantly.

Designing for printability meant thinking about wall thickness, infill orientation, and where to add fillets to prevent layer-splitting under load. The landing legs in particular went through multiple iterations — the final arched geometry distributes impact force along the curve rather than concentrating it at the mount points, while the material cutouts keep the mass down without compromising stiffness along the load path.

With full control over the geometry, the electronics could be routed intentionally rather than crammed in. Cable runs were planned in CAD before printing, and mounting points for the flight controller, GPS mast, ESCs, and battery were built directly into the frame.

04 — Leg Design

The most iterated part of the build

The landing leg looks simple. It wasn't. Early versions cracked at the mount points, splayed outward under lateral loads, or were too stiff and transmitted too much shock into the frame on hard landings. Each failure pointed to something — a wall too thin, a fillet too sharp, a geometry that looked fine in CAD but behaved differently once printed and stressed.

The final design uses an arched profile that naturally redirects impact forces along the curve toward the ground contact point, rather than bending the mount. The rectangular cutouts aren't decorative — they remove material from the sections that contribute least to bending stiffness, reducing mass without changing how the leg behaves under load. The geometry is informed by basic beam theory: keep material where the bending moment is highest, remove it where it isn't.

Printed in PETG at 40% gyroid infill with 4 perimeters. Final iteration survived all 10 drop accuracy trials without cracking.

Drop mechanism — CNC aluminum

05 — Drop Mechanism

Designed in CAD.
CNC'd in aluminum.

The payload release mechanism was the most mechanically precise part of the project. A servo-actuated latch holds the payload during flight and releases it on a software trigger when the drone reaches the target GPS coordinate. The geometry had to be tight enough to hold 4lbs securely through maneuvers, but release cleanly and consistently on command.

The mechanism was designed in SolidWorks with aluminum in mind — tolerances tight enough for a CNC operation, clearances sized for the specific servo horn geometry. The part was sent to a third-party CNC shop and came back ready to install. Designing for external manufacturing meant the drawings had to be unambiguous: correct tolerances, proper callouts, nothing left to interpretation.

Across 10 trials, the mechanism released cleanly every time. Combined with ArduPilot's waypoint trigger logic, it placed the payload within 0.5 meters of the GPS target consistently.
